coredata.dc:title: Reinforced built-in electric field and mediated Schottky barrier height via carbon quantum dots electronic bridges on BiOBr/Ti3C2 for efficient photocatalytic quinolone antibiotics degradation

coredata.dc:description: Schottky junction-based photocatalysts formed by metal-semiconductor contact are attractive, but their photocatalytic performance is limited by poor built-in electric field (IEF) driving force and excessive Schottky barrier height (SBH). A prospective strategy for efficient charge transfer is modulating interface gap states by inserting viable buffer layer. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were inserted in BiOBr/Ti3C2 Schottky heterojunction to form electronic bridges via Bi–O–C and Ti–O–C chemical bonds. The CQDs electronic bridges regulated charge spatial distribution, resulting in a 3.4-fold increased IEF, and facilitated efficient charge separation and transfer within BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. The carrier lifetime of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 had been extended to 2357.8 ps, increasing effectively charge carrier density. Besides, the reinforced interfacial interaction by Ti–C–O and Bi–C–O bonding significantly reduced the SBH from 2.02 eV to 1.77 eV within BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2, accelerating charge transport across the metal-semiconductor interface. Remarkably, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation for multiple quinolone antibiotics (FQs), especially for Moxifloxacin (MOX, 96.1 %) within 120 min, which was 2.63, 2.48 and 1.84 times higher than that of BiOBr, BiOBr/CQDs and BiOBr/Ti3C2, respectively. Furthermore, the high environmental adaptability and recycle stability were revealed in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. This work provides a new strategy to construct electronic bridges in Schottky-based photocatalysts for enhancing photocatalytic activities.

(b) TEM and (c–d) HRTEM images of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. (e–f) FTIR spectra of catalysts. High–resolution XPS spectra of (g) C1s, (h) Ti 2p and (i) Bi 4f for catalysts.

(c) The surface charge density, (d) IEF intensity (inset: open–circuit potentials) and (e) TPV spectra of BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. TA spectra signals of (b) BiOBr/Ti3C2 and (e) BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 with 340 nm excitation. TA decay dynamics of (c) BiOBr/Ti3C2 at 690 nm and (f) BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 at 698 nm.

Evolution of photocatalytic carrier transfer over BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. (a–b) The differential charge density and (c–d) ELF map of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. (e) The band structure of BiOBr, Ti3C2 and Ti3C2/CQDs. (f–g) Schematic diagram of SBH adjustment for facilitating electron transfer. (h) Active species capturing experiments of MOX removal. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schottky junction-based photocatalysts formed by metal-semiconductor contact are attractive, but their photocatalytic performance is limited by poor built-in electric field (IEF) driving force and excessive Schottky barrier height (SBH). A prospective strategy for efficient charge transfer is modulating interface gap states by inserting viable buffer layer. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were inserted in BiOBr/Ti3C2 Schottky heterojunction to form electronic bridges via Bi–O–C and Ti–O–C chemical bonds. The CQDs electronic bridges regulated charge spatial distribution, resulting in a 3.4-fold increased IEF, and facilitated efficient charge separation and transfer within BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. The carrier lifetime of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 had been extended to 2357.8 ps, increasing effectively charge carrier density. Besides, the reinforced interfacial interaction by Ti–C–O and Bi–C–O bonding significantly reduced the SBH from 2.02 eV to 1.77 eV within BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2, accelerating charge transport across the metal-semiconductor interface. Remarkably, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation for multiple quinolone antibiotics (FQs), especially for Moxifloxacin (MOX, 96.1 %) within 120 min, which was 2.63, 2.48 and 1.84 times higher than that of BiOBr, BiOBr/CQDs and BiOBr/Ti3C2, respectively. Furthermore, the high environmental adaptability and recycle stability were revealed in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. This work provides a new strategy to construct electronic bridges in Schottky-based photocatalysts for enhancing photocatalytic activities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The construction of metal-semiconductor heterojunction systems to enhance photocatalytic activity is a promising strategy that has attracted widespread attention. The Schottky contact is a crucial interface between semiconductors and metals, becoming increasingly significant in photocatalysts. Over the past decades, a range of Schottky-based photocatalysts, such as Pd/C3N4 [1], CdS/Ti3C2 [2], Bi/Bi2Sn2O7 [3], and BiOBr/Ti3C2 [4,5] have been extensively studied. The Schottky barrier, established at the metal–semiconductor junction, governs the transport of carriers across the interface. This barrier impedes the recombination of hot electrons with holes, thereby facilitating the photogenerated carrier separation efficiency [6,7]. However, the photocatalytic activity of Schottky-based photocatalysts is still unsatisfactory, attributed primarily to the inefficacious photo-induced carrier separation. These challenges arise from various factors: (1) Insufficient built-in electric field (IEF) strength to provide driving force to push electrons across the metal-semiconductor interface. (2) Adjusting the Schottky barrier height (SBH) is crucial for electron transfer, and an excessively large SBH increases carrier injection barriers, which inhibits high-energy electron separation. These limitations result in low photocatalytic efficiency and severely compromise the Schottky-based photocatalysts functionality.

Recently, several studies were conducted to enhance charge separation and transport of Schottky-based photocatalysts by precious metal doping [8], surface modification for semiconductor [9,10], and dynamic adjustment techniques (external electric field, light illumination, and piezotronics effect) [11–13]. These strategies had resulted in change the interface barrier, regulated charge distribution and improved charge transfer. Nevertheless, these approaches also tend to increase risk of leaching metal ions, pose significant synthetic challenges and result in high preparation costs [6]. Hence, a key objective is to design an efficient and settled method to fabricate Schottky-based photocatalysts with enhanced charge separation and transport, leading to excellent photocatalytic activity.

The improvement of photocatalytic performance for Schottky-based photocatalysts is believed to be significantly influenced by the interface gap states between metal and semiconductor. Inserting a viable buffer layer is conducive to create multi-electronic transport channel, which regulates the spatial separation of charge distribution [14,15]. Furthermore, lattice strain induced by buffer layer is beneficial in changing the energy band structure and lowering the potential barrier [16]. However, the actual interfacial charge transfer mechanism induced by adjusting interface gap states remains to be clarified. Among the candidates for constructing buffer layer, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel nanosized carbon materials with less than 10 nm in size, composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms for large π-conjugation systems, had excellent photoelectronic conversion capability and high electrical conductivity [17–19]. Specifically, when coupled to metals or semiconductors, CQDs-based photocatalysts could provide alternative carrier migration paths, thereby increasing charge carriers. Critically, CQDs was expected to form chemical bonds in metal-semiconductor interface due to its abundant adjustable functional groups [19]. Although CQDs had already been extensively explored in the literature act as electron acceptors or reservoir of photogenerated electrons for the photocatalytic degradation of various pharmaceuticals, the mechanism of constructing electronic bridges to regulate interfacial charge transfer by introducing CQDs was unclear. BiOBr/Ti3C2 Schottky heterojunction, confirmed in our previous study [4-5], was selected as photocatalyst due to its reliable stability and tunable electronic properties. However, it is still a great challengeable to regulate interface gap states and charge transfer to maximize the charge supply by BiOBr/Ti3C2 Schottky junction.

Herein, we rationally modulated the interface gap states of BiOBr/Ti3C2 Schottky heterojunction by inserting CQDs to form electronic bridges. This modulation aimed to adjust the interface charge distribution, thereby enhancing IEF driving force and reducing SBH. Experimental results demonstrated that CQDs electronic bridges markedly intensified the IEF intensity within BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 by 3.4 times and thus obviously accelerated charge transfer. Femtosecond transient absorption spectra (TAS) was performed to explore photogenerated charge dynamics, revealing that the carrier lifetime of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 had been extended to 2357.8 ps, increasing effectively charge carrier density. The lower SBH in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was attributed to reinforced interfacial interaction by Ti–C–O and Bi–C–O bonds. As a result, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 demonstrated excellent photocatalytic efficacy in degrading various quinolone antibiotics (FQs), achieving a remarkable 96 % elimination of Moxifloxacin (MOX). This work provides effective approach to improve the photocatalytic performance of Schottky-based photocatalysts by building electronic bridges, further reinforcing IEF and reducing SBH for efficient wastewater treatment.

All reagents were analytical grade and the corresponding detailed information could be found in Supplemental Material. The characterizations of the as-prepared catalysts were listed in Supplemental Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BiOBr, Ti3C2 and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: BiOBr and Ti3C2 were obtained based on our previous work. The detailed experimental procedures were shown in Supplemental Material. CQDs was prepared by a hydrothermal method. In a typical run, 3.0 g citrate and 1.0 g urea were dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water. Afterward, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling at ambient temperature, the suspension was centrifuged at 8000 r for 10 min to eliminate large unreacted particles. Then, the blackish green solution was dialyzed in a regenerated cellulose membrane (MWCO: 3500 Da) for 24 h and eventually vacuum freeze-dried to obtain solid blackish green CQDs.

BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was prepared by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Typically, 1.940 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 20 mL ethylene glycol solution (EG, concentration of 70 %), followed by adding 0.412 g NaBr to prepare BiOBr precursor (solution A). Meanwhile, solution B was obtained by dissolving 0.0366 g CQDs (3 wt%) and 0.122 g Ti3C2 (10 wt%) in 10 mL EG. Afterward, solution B was poured slowly into solution A, followed by magnetic stirring for 1 h. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 2 h. Eventually, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 powder was gained underwent washing and vacuum freeze-dried. For comparison, 0.5, 1, 2, 3 and 4 wt% CQDs were added in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 synthesis, respectively. Ti3C2/CQDs, BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs powder were obtained following the same process by excluding the addition of CQDs or Ti3C2.

Photocatalytic FQs antibiotics degradation was carried out in a 50 mL quartz tube, while a 500 W Xe lamp (λ > 420 nm, average light intensity = 257 W/m2) was used to activate the reaction. The photocatalytic performance was evaluated by degrading various FQs antibiotics, including Moxifloxacin (MOX), Lomefloxacin (LOM), Ofloxacin (OFL), Ciprofloxacin (CIP), Enrofloxacin (ENR), Norfloxacin (NOR) and Levofloxacin (LEV). Typically, 25 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL pollutant solutions (10 mg·L−1) and stirred in the dark for 30 min, followed by 2 h light irradiation. During the photocatalytic process, 2 mL reaction fluid was collected and analyzed by UV–visible spectrophotometer. The mineralization extent of FQs antibiotics after photocatalysis for 120 min was derived utilizing a TOC analyzer (TOC–LCPH). Specifically, the pH of the samples before and after catalysis was adjusted to about 3 with 0.05 mg·L−1 HCl, and the TOC content in the initial and post-catalysed solutions was determined. The TOC removal efficiencies were calculated as the organic matter mineralisation rates. Additionally, the procedure for assessing environmental factors and recycling activity were detailed in the Supplemental Material.

T.E.S.T. and microbiological toxicity testing were adopted to assess the biosecurity of MOX and intermediate degradation products. E.coli was chosen as an experimental strain to culture in Luria–Bertani culture medium at 37 °C for 24 h. The 100 μL of the sample reaction solutions were spread evenly on the culture medium surface. The culture medium without sample reaction solution was used as a control.

The differential charge density and electron localization function of BiOBr/Ti3C2, BiOBr/CQDs and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were calculated by Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) with the projector augmented wave (PAW) method. Moreover, Gaussian 16 W was adopted for the DFT calculation of MOX. The geometric structure optimization of MOX was obtained based on the B3LYP functional on the 6–31 G(d) basis. The condensed Fukui index basing the Hirshfeld charge and the electrostatic potential of MOX was calculated by Multiwfn version 3.8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CQDs electronic bridges on BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2
ce:section.ce:para: BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was prepared by a one-step microwave heating method, where its synthesis route was illustrated in Fig. 1 a. In this process, CQDs was coordinated with active –OH/−COOH and surface –O terminations to form Bi–O–C and Ti–O–C bonds, connecting BiOBr and Ti3C2. The TEM image of Figure S4a showed that the size of CQDs was distributed 1.00–5.00 nm and the average size was 3.13 nm. The polycrystalline CQDs displayed a lattice spacing of 0.247 nm to the (101) plane (Figure S4b) [20,21]. According to the results of SEM (Figure S4c), BiOBr exhibited a disorganized nanosheet stacking structure. Interestingly, BiOBr nanosheets grown in situ on Ti3C2 surface into compact 3D nanoflower–like spheres (Figure S4d). Following the introduction of CQDs, “petals” of BiOBr nanoflower–like spheres became sparser (Figure S4e–f). Additionally, the (002) peak at 9.1° (Ti3C2) shifted to a lower angle of 6.6° (Ti3C2/CQDs), where the corresponding basal spacing increased from 9.7 Å to 13.4 Å according to Bragg’s law (Figure S6a). The incorporation of CQDs has been shown to effectively increase the interlayer spacing of both BiOBr sheets and Ti3C2 layers, thereby facilitating a stronger interaction between the constituent components. Heterostructure composed of polycrystalline CQDs, BiOBr and Ti3C2 layer could be observed in TEM of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (Fig. 1b–c). The lattice stripes at 0.278, 0.262 and 0.324 nm in HRTEM image were attributed to BiOBr (110), Ti3C2 (0110) and CQDs (002) crystal planes, which proved the successful construction of heterojunction (Fig. 1d). Noteworthy, CQDs acted as bridges and were in close contact with BiOBr and Ti3C2 at both ends respectively. The FTIR spectra of BiOBr/Ti3C2, BiOBr/CQDs and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were match well to the characteristic vibration modes of BiOBr, with a characteristic peak of Bi–O at 510 cm−1 (Fig. 1e) [22–24]. In addition, two new peaks at 873 and 790 cm−1 after CQDs addition, further confirmed the existence of Bi–C–O and Ti–O–C bonds in the BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 heterojunction (Fig. 1e–f) [25–28]. As the proportion of CQDs increased, the strength of H–O and C–H bonds increased, which provided an abundance of surface functional groups. The combined forms of BiOBr, CQDs, and Ti3C2 were further characterized using XPS spectra. XPS survey spectra (0 ∼ 1300 eV) confirmed that BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was composited with Bi, Br, O, Ti and C elements (Figure S8a). The XPS peak intensities for both Ti3C2 and CQDs were relatively subdued as a result of their minimal incorporation levels. Upon the successful incorporation of CQDs, the O1s peak intensity in Ti3C2 was notably amplified, whereas the F1s peak experienced a substantial attenuation (Figure S8c). Based on the relative intensities of the O 1 s and F 1 s peaks, the termination of Ti3C2 to be 43.79 % =O, 11.7 % −OH, 44.51 % −F and Ti3C2/CQDs with 67.32 % =O, 5.43 % −OH, 27.32 % −F (Figure S8f). This observation suggested that the implantation of CQDs effectively promoted the formation of Ti–O bonds and reduced the content of Ti–F bonds, which provided favorable conditions for the construction of electronic bridges between Ti3C2 and CQDs. This argument was strongly supported by evidence from the high-resolution C 1 s, Ti 2p and O 1 s spectrum of Ti3C2/CQDs. In Fig. 1g–h and Figure S8b, the XPS analysis of Ti3C2/CQDs revealed that the material retained the peak morphology of Ti3C2. Additionally, three new peaks appeared at 288.5 eV (C–O–Ti), 464.4 eV (Ti–O–C), and 531.5 eV (C = O/C–O–Ti), which could be attributed to the strong interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs and the Ti atoms on the surface of Ti3C2 [28,29]. This interaction facilitated electron transport at the interface between Ti3C2 and CQDs. In the high-resolution XPS of Bi 4f, all samples displayed tangible Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 peaks (Fig. 1i). Additionally, two new peaks appeared at the binding energies of 158.0 and 163.5 eV, indicating the establishment of Bi–O–C bonds between BiOBr and CQDs [30]. Collectively, these characterizations conclusively confirmed the CQDs electronic bridges structure on BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 via Bi–O–C and Ti–O–C bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reinforced built-in electric field and mediated Schottky barrier height mechanisms via CQDs electronic bridges
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the enhancement of IEF between BiOBr and Ti3C2 by CQDs, Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) was applied for visual evidence on charge transfer pathway. The heights of BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were respectively obtained as 1.7 and 1.1 μm, demonstrating that CQDs does not change the original morphology of BiOBr/Ti3C2 (Fig. 2 a). In KPFM of BiOBr/Ti3C2 (Fig. 2b), a pronounced disparity was observed in the surface potential between the central and marginal regions, with the central exhibiting a significant elevation (ΔE = 48.34 mV). This discrepancy was indicative of an IEF oriented from BiOBr towards Ti3C2, serving as a propellant for the directional transfer of photogenerated charges [31]. After the introduction of CQDs, a higher surface potential difference (ΔE = 61.12 mV) was obtained in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. The enhancement of interface surface potential revealed that CQDs was an electron bridging, as the electrons of BiOBr migrated to Ti3C2, leading to an increased IEF intensity. The driving force magnitude of IEF was estimated by surface charge density and surface voltage according to the model of Kanata–Kito et al. [32]. In Fig. 2c, the surface charge density of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (33.27 μC·cm−2) was higher than that of BiOBr/Ti3C2 (12.89 μC·cm−2), which indicated more charge accumulation on BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. The surface voltages of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (0.520 V) and BiOBr/Ti3C2 (0.395 V) were obtained by open–circuit potential measurements (Fig. 2d inset). Thus, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 exhibited 3.4 times stronger IEF than BiOBr/Ti3C2, significantly promoting charge transfer (Fig. 2d). The efficacy of separation and directional migration of photogenerated carriers were measured utilizing transient-state surface photovoltage (TPV), time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) and steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The TPV relaxation curves (Fig. 2e) suggested a little effect on the charge extraction rate with the similar maximum charge extraction time (tmax) of BiOBr/Ti3C2 (0.025 ms) and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (0.063 ms). BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 exhibited greater charge extraction (S, 0.0475 ms·mV) and attenuation constant (τ, 0.273 ms) than that of BiOBr/Ti3C2 (S, 0.0231 ms·mV and τ, 0.211 ms), which indicated that BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 had a slower carriers recombination rate than that of BiOBr/Ti3C2 [33–35]. TRPL decay spectroscopy (Fig. 2f) suggested that the charge carrier lifetime at 467 nm of BiOBr, BiOBr/CQDs and BiOBr/Ti3C2 were respectively 0.062, 0.120 and 0.249 μs, which was extended to 0.314 μs of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. Meanwhile, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 displayed the lowest PL emission peak intensity, while lower fluorescence intensity implies lower recombination rate, which indicated that the enhanced IEF interactions among the catalysts had effectively augmented the efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation (Fig. 2g). Besides, multiple PL peaks belonged to exciton focusing, mainly from oxygen vacancy defects on the BiOBr surface, which was consistent with the XPS results [36]. Therefore, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 had better photocurrent responsiveness and lower charge transfer impedance. Upon visible light irradiation (Fig. 2h), BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 showed a highest average photocurrent density of 0.60 μA·cm−3, which was notably higher than BiOBr/CQDs (0.16 μA·cm−3), BiOBr/Ti3C2 (0.13 μA·cm−3) and BiOBr (0.09 μA·cm−3), respectively. Upon near-infrared light irradiation, the clear photocurrent curve of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs were obtained, which could be attributed to the up-converted fluorescence feature for CQDs, leading to improving the utilization of full spectrum light [37,38]. Besides, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 through EIS spectra was the lowest, confirming its best conductivity (Figure S9). These experimental results fully evidenced that the IEF reinforced by CQDs was instrumental in facilitating the migration of photocarriers, decelerating the recombination process, and extending the lifetime of charge carriers.

In order to ulteriorly understand charge-transfer mechanism by IEF, TAS spectra was performed to explore photogenerated charge dynamics. Instantly after pump pulse (λ = 320 nm) excitation, BiOBr/Ti3C2 appeared with a positive excited-state absorption (ESA) peak at 490 nm and a ground-state bleach (GSB) peak at 690 nm, which represented the excited state process and relaxation (Fig. 3 a–b) [39]. Except for the ESA and GSB peaks, a stimulated emission (SE) peak at 645 nm could be observed in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2, which was the signal generated by the rapid electron–hole recombination induced by the IEF through the CQDs (Fig. 3d–e). In addition, an apparent peak at 760 nm in BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was ascribed to the photoinduced absorption (PA) band, while this peak was vague in BiOBr/Ti3C2. This result indicated that this peak could be interpreted as a photoinduced emission process originating from the enhanced interfacial charge transfer by CQDs [40]. Moreover, the decay kinetics of BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 at 690 and 698 nm were fitted with multi-exponential models. Commonly, τ1, τ2, and τ3 respectively corresponded to the electron capture process, interface electron transferred process and recombination process of holes and impurity electrons, while A denoted the photo-generated electrons proportion [41]. In Fig. 3c and f, compared to the τ1 (5.6 ps) of BiOBr/Ti3C2, the increased τ1 (24.9 ps) of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 demonstrated the facilitating the electron transfer to the surface trap level [42]. The τ2 (201.2 ps) and τ3 (2650.1 ps) of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were much higher than τ2 (107.8 ps) and τ3 (1670.3 ps) of BiOBr/Ti3C2, which implied the enhanced IEF facilitated photogenerated electron-hole separation and rapid interfacial electron transfer through CQDs. Therefore, the τave carrier lifetime of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 had been extended to 2357.8 ps. The results above sufficiently authenticated the accelerated charge separation in enhanced IEF by CQDs.

Identification of electron transfer direction was pivotal for elucidating the electron transfer process. Thus, the surface electronic interactions of different elements were explored by XPS. In Figure S8c, the high-resolution XPS of O 1 s for BiOBr could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.9, 531.7 and 533.2 eV, corresponding to the Bi–O, oxygen vacancies (OVs) and O–H, respectively [43]. When the addition of CQDs, the substantial enhancement of oxygen vacancies could be ascribed to hydrogen bonding interactions interfering with the Bi–O bonds [44]. In the high–resolution XPS of Bi 4f (Fig. 1i), compared with BiOBr, the binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 for BiOBr/Ti3C2 were negatively shifted with 0.1 eV, denoting electronic transfer from BiOBr to Ti3C2. Morover, 0.8 ∼ 1.2 eV negative shift was observed in that of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2, indicating that CQDs facilitate electron transfer BiOBr to Ti3C2. In Figure S8d, compared with BiOBr, 0.3 ∼ 0.4 eV (BiOBr/Ti3C2) and 0.9 ∼ 1.1 eV (BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2) negative shift of Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 binding energies also verified direction of electron transfer from BiOBr to Ti3C2 via CQDs. The differential charge density of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 calculated by Density functional theory (DFT) demonstrated electron redistribution at the interface of Ti3C2/CQDs and BiOBr/CQDs, with electronic accumulation on the top of Ti3C2 and bottom of BiOBr (Fig. 4 a–b). The electron localization function (ELF) map further confirmed the influence of CQDs on the electron behavior, while the red and blue regions indicated the lattice electron and active covalent electron, respectively (Fig. 4c–d). The Bi–O and Ti–O bonds could induce the formation of delocalized electrons around C atoms on CQDs, which further proved strong interaction at the BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 interface. These results indicated that CQDs as an electronic bridge to accelerate electron transfer from BiOBr to Ti3C2.

To deepen understanding of the charge transfer mechanism within vis–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system, the energy band structure underwent meticulous examination. The band gap energy (Eg) of BiOBr, BiOBr/CQDs, BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were respectively determined to be 2.45, 2.25, 2.11 and 1.66 eV according to the Kubelka–Munk formula based on UV–vis DRS (Figure S10b) [45]. BiOBr was corroborated to be an n-type semiconductor due to its positive slope of the Mott–Schottky plots under 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 kHz, while its conduction band potential (ECB) was determined to be −0.41 V (Figure S12) [22,44]. Further the valence band (EVB) was calculated as 2.04 V according to the formula EVB = Eg–ECB. The Fermi energy levels (Ef) of BiOBr, Ti3C2, Ti3C2/CQDs, BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were estimated as 5.28, 6.05, 5.80, 5.84 and 5.66 eV by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) based on the formula Φ = hv–Ecut−off (Figure S13) [24]. The staggered-band structures between BiOBr and co-catalyst (Ti3C2 or Ti3C2/CQDs) determined the formation of heterojunctions and IEF (Fig. 4e). Due to the difference of Ef, the energy band of BiOBr bent upward and formed Schottky barrier at the interface of BiOBr/Ti3C2, effectively inhibiting electron backflow (Fig. 4f) [3,4]. However, the overly large SBH imposed constraints on electron transfer across the metal–semiconductor interface [6]. The ΦSBH of BiOBr/Ti3C2 and BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were measured as 2.02 and 1.77 eV according to the formula ΦSBH = Φmetal–χ [7]. Thus, when BiOBr was excited by light irradiation to produce photogenerated electrons, the lower ΦSBH between BiOBr and Ti3C2/CQDs, as well as enhanced IEF in space charge region were all beneficial for BiOBr electrons to be driven to Ti3C2, leading to effectively electron separation and migration (Fig. 4g). The effectively isolated photogenerated carriers were used to participate in the generation of reactive radicals. In Fig. 4h, the results of capturing experiments indicated that ·O2 − and ·OH played pivotal roles in MOX degradation. And e− and h+ played an indispensable role in photocatalytic process due to their involvement in the production and transformation of ·O2 − and ·OH. The EPR technology further identified the prominent radicals produced in vis-BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system. As displayed in Fig. 4i–j, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 exhibited the strongest EPR signal peaks of DMPO−·O2 − and DMPO−·OH under 10 min irradiation, which indicated the ability of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 to produce ·O2 − and ·OH was significantly improved. Additionally, the intensity of DMPO−·O2 − and DMPO−·OH of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 incrementally increased with extended light irradiation times (0, 1 and 10 min) in Figure S14. This underscored the ability of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 to produce ROS continually under light irradiation. Based on the band structures in Fig. 4e, the EVB values of BiOBr was measured as 2.04 V, which was lower than the standard redox potential of H2O/·OH (2.48 V), and therefore it could not oxidize H2O to ·OH. Additionally, there was a significant difference between the contributions of ·OH and h+ according to capturing experiments, proving only a small fraction of ·OH came from the conversion of h+. Hence, the detected ESR signal of DMPO−·OH should originate from the conversion of ·O2 − (Equation (1)–(3)). And h+ was involved in the conversion of a small amount of ·OH since the EVB values of BiOBr (2.04 V) was higher than that of OH−/·OH (1.99 V) (Equation (4).

Herin, the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism over BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was proposed in Fig. 5 . Under irradiation, CQDs as energy converters to expand the light utilization due to its up-converted fluorescence feature. The photoexcited electrons migrated towards CB of BiOBr, producing holes in VB. Owing to the tight connection by Bi–O–C and Ti–O–C bonds, CQDs formed as electronic bridge at interface between BiOBr and Ti3C2. Driven by the enhanced IEF in space charge region, the photogenerated electrons rapidly migrated from BiOBr to Ti3C2 surface via CQDs channel. Besides, the lower ΦSBH not only effectively suppressed electron backflow, but also facilitated more energetic electrons to cross the interface barrier. The electrons distributed on the Ti3C2 surface and holes accumulated on the VB of BiOBr further react respectively with O2 and H2O, producing ample ·O2 − and ·OH. Ultimately, multi FQs antibiotics (MOX, LOM, CIP, ENR, LEV, NOR and OFL) were efficiently reacted to generate CO2, H2O, and small molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced photocatalytic performance of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 for FQs antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of vis–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system was evaluated with MOX as target pollutant. Firstly, the dark–adsorption experiments of MOX were investigated in 60 min (Figure S16a), and all the samples arrived at the adsorption–desorption equilibrium within 30 min. Ti3C2 could adsorb 10.2 % MOX due to its abundance of surface functional groups, while the termination of Ti3C2 to be 43.79 % =O, 11.7 % −OH, 44.51 % −F (Figure S8f) [46]. In Fig. 6 a, the pristine BiOBr exhibited unsatisfactory photocatalytic efficiency for MOX degradation (69.2 %). MOX degradation of BiOBr/Ti3C2 (82.3 %) was enhanced by the formation of a heterojunction between BiOBr and Ti3C2. Compared to that of BiOBr, MOX degradation of BiOBr/CQDs was only slightly improved (72.5 %), indicating that CQDs could not directly enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr. Notably, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 showed the highest MOX degradation (96.1 %), which was attributed to the use of CQDs as a medium for the fast transport of electrons. The corresponding kinetic constant k for MOX degradation of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was 0.02635 min−1, which was 37.64, 2.63, 2.48 and 1.84 times higher than that of Ti3C2 (0.0007 min−1), BiOBr (0.01001 min−1), BiOBr/CQDs (0.01063 min−1) and BiOBr/Ti3C2 (0.01433 min−1), respectively (Fig. 6b). In UV–visible spectrum, the maximum absorbance peak of MOX (288 nm) weakened gradually via the dark adsorption and photocatalysis process, denoting the fragmentation and degradation of MOX (Fig. 6c). Additionally, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 could effectively degrade diverse FQs antibiotics, and the degradation rates of LEV, NOR, ENR, CIP, OFL, LOM and MOX could reach 86.3 %, 87.8 %, 87.4 %, 88.6 %, 89.3 %, 91.2 % and 96.1 %, respectively (Fig. 6e). The corresponding TOC removal of LEV, NOR, ENR, CIP, OLF, LOM and MOX were respectively up to 62.1 %, 65.5 %, 64.5 %, 69.3 %, 64.5 %, 60.3 % and 67.2 %, indicating efficient mineralization capacity of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (Fig. 6f). Furthermore, the photocatalytic efficacy of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 was advantageous compared to previously documented photocatalysts, highlighting its potential for antibiotic contaminants degradation in wastewater (Table S9). It proved new BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 via novel mechanisms of introducing CQDs as electric bridge to reinforce IEF driving force and reduce Schottky barrier height, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic activity and effectively degrading FQs antibiotic.

To assess the adaptability of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2, multiple environmental factors were considered for their impacts on photocatalytic processes. It could be seen from Figure S16c that HA at 1 to 20 mg·L−1 had little influence on MOX degradation by BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. The inhibition of catalytic efficiency at 30 mg·L−1 HA was due to the role of light shielding by superset HA [47]. BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 remained excellent photocatalytic performance when the initial pH ranges from 5 to 9 (Figure S16d). Meanwhile, BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 could still respectively achieved 60.3 % and 73.6 % MOX removal efficiency at pH of 3 and 11, indicating its adaptability to extreme acidic and alkaline environments. Some common anions (Na+, Cl−, HCO3 − and SO4 2−) had little effect on MOX degradation, while NH4 +, Ca2+, NO3 − and CO3 2− exhibited slightly inhibition (Figure S16e). This decline in photocatalytic efficacy could be attributed to the shielding of active sites on the BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 surface by Ca2+, and the scavenging of active species by NH4 +, NO3 − and CO3 2− [48]. The MOX degradation rates in lake water, tap water and river water were respectively recorded at 86.4 %, 84.6 % and 83.7 %, suggesting a marginal inhibition of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2. However, elevated ionic concentration in seawater produced competition adsorption at active sites, resulting in unsatisfactory MOX degradation (38.9 %) (Figure S16f) [49]. Furthermore, the reusability and stability of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 were explored through a series of five consecutive dark adsorption and photocatalytic degradation cycles (Fig. 6d). After 5 cycles, the MOX adsorption experienced a significant decrease from 31.9 % to 17.5 %, likely due to the accrual of residual MOX and intermediate products on the BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 surface. Encouragingly, MOX removal exhibited a mere 1.54 % decrease after each cycle, indicating that BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 retained robust photocatalytic performance. Additionally, the XRD and FTIR analyses revealed consistent characteristic peaks for both fresh and utilized BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (Figure S18a–b). The XPS analysis showed no significant difference in the Ti2p peaks of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 before and after using (Figure S18c). The FESEM images validated the preservation of morphological integrity for BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 (Figure S18d–e), likely owing to the robust Ti–O–C and Bi–O–C bond formation. ICP–MS assessment detected Bi ion leaching in the MOX–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system, registering a concentration of 3.2 μg·L−1. The result confirmed the absence of secondary pollution within the vis–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system. Overall, the above results demonstrated the commendable environmental adaptability, cyclic stability and practical application potential of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2.

The intermediates and potential degradation pathways of MOX were delineated through the application of Fukui function calculations and HPLC-MS analysis. The electrostatic potential map (ESP) of MOX indicated that the regions around 3O in the carbonyl group, 5O in the carboxyl group and 7 N in the piperazine ring were easily attacked by ·O2 − (Figure S19). The calculated isosurfaces of Fukui electrophilic index (f − ) and nucleophilic index (f + ) visually described the electron density change of MOX. According to Fukui function calculation (Table S8), the 21C, 23C, 24C, 27C and 28C positions exhibited high f + values, implying prone to be attacked by ·O2 −; the 2O, 3O, 4O, 5O, 6 N and 7 N positions displayed high f − values, suggesting the potential for h+ attack; the 6 N position proved high f 0 value, which could be attacked by ·OH [50]. The intermediates were further identified by HPLC–MS at several reaction processes (0, 30, 60, 90 and 120 min), with the corresponding mass spectra and chromatograms concisely summarized in Figure S20. Afterward, three probable photodegradation pathways for MOX were delineated in Fig. 7 a. In pathway 1, MOX first generated A1 through defluorination followed loosed carbonyl group to form A2. Then, ring–opening reaction and decarboxylation occurred to generate A3 and A4 [51]. In pathway 2, the quinolone ring of MOX was attacked by ·O2 − and ·OH to form B1 and further oxidized to generate B2 and B3 [52]. In pathway 3, C1 was obtained by oxidation of the piperazine ring on MOX. Then, demethylation and defluorination occurred to form C3 and C4 [53]. Eventually, these intermediate products further cleavage and oxidize to be mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

The biosecurity and environmental risks associated with the MOX degradation process were assessed using Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.). The acute toxicity daphnia magna LC50–48 h and fathead minnows LC50–96 h, bioconcentration factor and mutagenicity index were selected as evaluation indicators, while the consensus method as forecasting methodology [54]. In comparison with MOX, most intermediates displayed higher LC50 values of daphnia magna and fathead minnows, indicating effective reduction of biotoxicity during MOX degradation (Fig. 7b–c). And most intermediates exhibited lower bioconcentration factor and mutagenicity index than MOX, which meant lower risk of ecological enrichment and biological aberration (Fig. 7d–e). The antibacterial activity of MOX and its photodegraded intermediates against E.coli were calculated by spread plate method [55]. As shown in Fig. 7f–g, the addition of MOX could completely inhibit the growth of E.coli. In the same conditions, the addition of BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 leaching solution had little effect on E.coli growth, while its E.coli strain counts were close to the blank sample. This result showed that BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 could not only degrade MOX efficiently but also mitigate its ecotoxicity.

In summary, we provided a new strategy for building electronic bridges in BiOBr/Ti3C2 Schottky junction and further proposed the interfacial charge transfer mechanism enhanced by CQDs electronic bridges. The 3.4–fold increased IEF at the BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 interface redistributed the charge space and provided the driving force for charge transport. The Ti–C–O and Bi–C–O bonds strengthened the interfacial interactions, greatly reducing the SBH from 2.02 eV to 1.77 eV within BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 and accelerating the charge transport. The vis–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system enhanced ·O2 − and ·OH active species generation, thereby increasing oxidation capacity for pollutants. Hence, vis–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system had excellent photocatalytic removal of FQs antibiotics and high tolerance to complex water disturbances. Moreover, the vis–BiOBr/CQDs/Ti3C2 system could effectively reduce the biotoxicity of MOX and its intermediates. This work provides new insights into designing efficient Schottky–based photocatalysts for disposing of environmental pollution and extending potential photocatalysis applications.
